name: QuantLib-Python (Quantitative Finance Library)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-3-Clause); Luigi Ballabio / QuantLib community

performance.notes: C++ SWIG bindings; fast for derivatives pricing; Monte Carlo can be slow

agent_readiness.notes: Comprehensive quantitative finance library. import QuantLib as ql. today = ql.Date(15, 3, 2024). ql.Settings.instance().evaluationDate = today. calendar = ql.UnitedStates(ql.UnitedStates.NYSE). Option pricing: option = ql.EuropeanOption(ql.PlainVanillaPayoff(ql.Option.Call, 100), ql.EuropeanExercise(maturity)). spot = ql.SimpleQuote(100). rTS = ql.YieldTermStructure... process = ql.BlackScholesMertonProcess(ql.QuoteHandle(spot), ...). option.setPricingEngine(ql.AnalyticEuropeanEngine(process)). option.NPV(). option.delta(). Bonds: ql.FixedRateBond. Swaps: ql.VanillaSwap. Yield curves: ql.PiecewiseLogLinearDiscount. Interest rate models: Vasicek, Hull-White. Monte Carlo engines.

gotchas: Steep learning curve — requires finance domain knowledge (LIBOR, discount curves, etc.)
SWIG bindings — Python API mirrors C++ which is not Pythonic
EvaluationDate must be set before any computation
Documentation is sparse for Python — C++ docs are primary reference
No type annotations — autocomplete/IDE support poor
best_when: Derivatives pricing: European/American options, bonds, swaps, caps/floors
Interest rate model calibration (Vasicek, Hull-White, CIR)
Risk metrics: Greeks (delta, gamma, vega), duration, convexity
summary: QuantLib-Python prices derivatives and fixed income instruments. EuropeanOption with AnalyticEuropeanEngine. option.NPV(), .delta(), .gamma(). Yield curve bootstrapping. VanillaSwap, FixedRateBond. Interest rate models (Hull-White, Vasicek). Monte Carlo engines. BSD-3 by Luigi Ballabio. Gold standard for academic/quant finance. Steep learning curve. Non-Pythonic SWIG bindings. EvaluationDate must be set. No type annotations.
